wagner2023_openness_impact.research_question: Does international collaboration and researcher mobility correlate with higher scientific impact, and how can this be measured?
conclusion_claims: Openness, as measured by international collaboration and researcher mobility, is strongly correlated with scientific impact.
The findings have important implications for policy making around investment in R&D and the flows of students, researchers, and technical workers.
limitations: The study is limited to OECD countries and does not include non-OECD countries.
The data on public spending on international collaborations is not directly available and must be inferred.

partial_results.note: Results were similar for both methods in terms of correlations and model explanatory power.

abramo2017_stardom.title: How long do top scientists maintain their stardom? An analysis by region, gender and discipline: evidence from Italy
abramo2017_stardom.research_question: How long do top scientists maintain their stardom, and how does this vary by discipline, gender, and region?
abramo2017_stardom.data_source: Ministry of Education, Universities and Research (MIUR) database and the Observatory of Public Research (ORP) database.
conclusion_claims: More than a third of the nation’s top scientists maintain their status over three consecutive periods.
The longevity of stardom varies by discipline, gender, and region, with implications for national policies on academic recruitment and advancement.
limitations: The study is limited to Italian professors in the hard sciences, so the findings may not generalize to other countries or disciplines.
The study does not provide a detailed statistical analysis of the factors influencing the longevity of stardom.

result_claims.metric: percentage of top scientists maintaining stardom
longevity of stardom in life sciences

result_claims.value: females are less likely to maintain top status
less likely to survive in southern Italy

partial_results.note: The study investigates the longevity of unproductive professors, but the results are not fully detailed in the abstract.

nikonova2022_measuring_difficulty.title: Measuring Difficulty of Novelty Reaction
nikonova2022_measuring_difficulty.research_question: How can the difficulty of novelty reaction for AI systems be measured or approximated before the agent interacts with it?
conclusion_claims: The proposed method for measuring novelty reaction difficulty aligns with the observed performance of AI agents in most cases.
The method is practical and can be applied to other domains where novelty difficulty is measured by solution space differences.
limitations: The method relies on random sampling agents, which introduces randomness and potential misclassification.
The limited number of sampled levels may affect the accuracy of difficulty predictions.

partial_results.note: These novelties were misclassified due to the randomness of the sampling agents or limited number of sampled levels.

shekar2022_novelty_based.title: Novelty-based Generalization Evaluation for Traffic Light Detection
shekar2022_novelty_based.research_question: How can the generalization ability of CNNs in autonomous driving applications be evaluated by considering the novelty of test samples?
shekar2022_novelty_based.data_source: Bosch Small Traffic light dataset, DriveU dataset
conclusion_claims: The proposed framework provides a more accurate evaluation of CNN generalization by incorporating the novelty of test samples.
Using VAEs for dimensionality reduction and novelty scoring improves the interpretability of the evaluation.
limitations: The results are specific to the traffic light detection application and may not generalize to other domains.
The novelty scoring approach may not capture all aspects of data novelty.

result_claims.value: Higher scores indicate better performance on novel data
Higher scores indicate more novel test samples

partial_results.note: The results are specific to the traffic light detection application and may not generalize to other domains.

conclusion_claims: Citation structural diversity is a promising metric for evaluating literature, offering advantages in capturing interdisciplinarity and long-term academic influence.

limitations: The paper does not specify the exact datasets or databases used for the analysis.
The methodology for integrating semantic information is not detailed in the abstract.

partial_results.note: The exact relationship between structural diversity and citation volume/impact is not quantified in the abstract.

gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.title: Quantifying interdisciplinary synergy in higher STEM education
gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.research_question: How can interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula be quantified and evaluated to support the design of integrated educational programs?
gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.data_source: Higher Education in Korea portal operated by the Ministry of Education in Korea
available_fields: Mathematics, Physics, Astronomy, and Earth Science
conclusion_claims: Engineering fields constitute the core structure of interdisciplinary curriculum design, while basic natural science fields serve as peripheral stems.
The proposed framework provides a principled method for quantifying and evaluating interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula.
limitations: The study is limited to data from Korea and may not be generalizable to other countries with different educational systems.
The analysis is based on department-level data, which may not capture finer distinctions at the program or course level.

result_claims.value: Higher for combinations within or across closely related fields, especially in engineering fields.
Higher in engineering fields compared to basic natural sciences.

partial_results.note: The results are based on a specific dataset from Korea and may not generalize to other educational systems.

buruk2023_academic_writing.title: Academic Writing with GPT-3.5: Reflections on Practices, Efficacy and Transparency
buruk2023_academic_writing.research_question: How can GPT-3.5 be effectively and transparently used in academic writing, and what are its impacts on writing practices and ethics?
conclusion_claims: GPT-3.5 can be used in academic writing in five distinct ways: Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and Polisher.
The use of GPT-3.5 in academic writing raises ethical concerns, including plagiarism, misinformation, and transparency, which need to be addressed for responsible use.
limitations: The study is based on a single author's personal experience and is not generalizable.
The paper does not include quantitative analysis or comparative studies of GPT-3.5's efficacy across different writing tasks.

conclusion_claims: The University of Lagos has shown a consistent increase in research productivity over the two-decade period, with 2023 recording the highest publication output.

partial_results.note: The exact magnitude of the impact of collaboration on citation rates is not quantified in the paper.

thelwall2025_openalex_citation.title: Is OpenAlex Suitable for Research Quality Evaluation and Which Citation Indicator is Best?
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.research_question: Which citation-based indicator and database (OpenAlex or Scopus) best correlates with research quality as judged by expert evaluations?
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.data_source: OpenAlex, Scopus, and UK Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021 data
conclusion_claims: OpenAlex is suitable for citation analysis in most fields.
Raw citation counts are at least as good as field-normalized indicators for evaluating research quality.
limitations: The study is limited to UK REF2021 articles and may not generalize to other regions or fields.
The use of ChatGPT as a gold standard may introduce biases or inaccuracies.

result_claims.value: at least as good as field-normalized indicators

partial_results.note: Field normalization may be ineffective because more cited fields tend to produce higher quality work, affecting interdisciplinary research or within-field topic differences.

park2023_grants_interdisciplinary_advanc.title: Interdisciplinary Papers Supported by Disciplinary Grants Garner Deep and Broad Scientific Impact
park2023_grants_interdisciplinary_advanc.research_question: Do interdisciplinary grants support high-impact interdisciplinary advances?
conclusion_claims: Interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants tend to have higher impact than those supported by interdisciplinary grants.
Disciplinary grants play an important role in producing high-impact interdisciplinary advances.
limitations: The study does not fully explain why interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants have higher impact.
The results are based on a specific time period (1985-2009) and may not generalize to more recent years.

result_claims.value: Higher for interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants
Lower for papers supported by interdisciplinary grants

partial_results.note: The impact of interdisciplinary papers supported by interdisciplinary grants is lower than expected, but the reason for this is not fully explained.

maddi2022_article_processing_charges.title: Article Processing Charges, Altmetrics and Citation Impact: Is there an economic rationale?
maddi2022_article_processing_charges.research_question: Does paying high APCs necessarily increase the impact of scientific publications, and what factors determine APCs?
maddi2022_article_processing_charges.data_source: OpenAPC database, Web of Science (OST in-house database), Altmetric.com database
conclusion_claims: Paying high APCs does not necessarily increase the impact of scientific publications.
Hybrid journals charge significantly higher APCs than full OA journals.
Altmetrics do not have a strong impact on the visibility and citation impact of OA articles.
limitations: The study is based on a specific set of databases and may not be generalizable to all OA publications.
The analysis is descriptive and does not include causal inference methods.

result_claims.metric: Correlation between APCs and citation impact

result_claims.value: 50% higher than in full OA journals

result_claims.metric: Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact

result_claims.value: More correlated with classic bibliometric indicators

partial_results.metric: Correlation between APCs and citation impact
partial_results.note: The correlation is moderate, suggesting that higher APCs do not necessarily lead to higher impact.

partial_results.metric: Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact
partial_results.note: The correlation is lower than expected, indicating that Altmetrics may not be a strong predictor of citation impact.

thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.title: Do altmetric scores reflect article quality? Evidence from the UK Research Excellence Framework 2021
thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.research_question: How useful are altmetric scores as indicators of research quality in all fields, and how do they compare to citation counts?
thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.data_source: UK Research Excellence Framework 2021 (REF2021) peer review scores, Altmetric.com, Mendeley, and Scopus.
conclusion_claims: Altmetric scores are better indicators of research quality than previously thought, though not as strong as citation counts.
Mendeley reader counts are the strongest altmetric indicator of quality in most fields, followed by tweet counts and Altmetric Attention Score.
limitations: Field-normalized citation counts may not always be the best indicator in single fields.
Low numbers of articles in arts and humanities UoAs may affect precision.
Altmetric data may not be complete for all DOIs, especially for preprints or alternative versions.

result_claims.metric: Correlation between Mendeley reader count and quality score
Correlation between tweet count and quality score
Correlation between Altmetric Attention Score and quality score
result_claims.value: Almost as strong as Mendeley reader count

result_claims.metric: Correlation between field-normalized citation count and quality score
result_claims.value: Stronger than altmetrics in most fields

result_claims.metric: Correlation between raw citation count and quality score

partial_results.note: May reduce its strength as a quality indicator in single fields.
Moderately strong in some UoAs but not consistently across all fields.

bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.title: Industry Influence in High-Profile Social Media Research
bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.research_question: To what extent is social media research independent from industry influence?
bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.data_source: OpenAlex, Altmetrics, and manually validated data from industry-announced RFPs and fellowships.
conclusion_claims: Industry influence in social media research is extensive, impactful, and often opaque.
There is a need to strengthen disclosure norms and implement policies to ensure the visibility of independent research and the integrity of industry-supported research.
limitations: The study focuses on a relatively small corpus of high-profile research, which may not be representative of the broader field.
The analysis relies on public data and manual validation, which may not capture all industry ties.

result_claims.value: Twice the impact of independent work

result_claims.metric: Undisclosed ties among authors with no declared competing interests

partial_results.note: Unclear whether due to data access, PR campaigns, or other factors.

purkayastha2026_reviewing_reviewer.title: Reviewing the Reviewer: Elevating Peer Review Quality through LLM-Guided Feedback
purkayastha2026_reviewing_reviewer.research_question: How can peer review quality be improved by detecting and addressing lazy thinking and specificity issues in review segments using LLMs?
conclusion_claims: The proposed neuro-symbolic issue detection approach outperforms the strongest LLM-only model in detecting lazy thinking and specificity issues.
The LLM-driven feedback generation module improves the constructiveness and relevance of feedback by about 20% over the best baselines.
The LAZYREVIEWPLUS dataset is the first multi-label review dataset annotated with lazy thinking and specificity issues for issue identification and feedback generation.
limitations: The experimental section is relatively weak, with only the Gemma model being tested in detail.
The paper does not provide a comprehensive comparison of all tested models (e.g., GPT-4) in the abstract.

kuznetsov2026_peer_review.title: Is Peer Review Really in Decline? Analyzing Review Quality across Venues and Time
kuznetsov2026_peer_review.research_question: Is there a consistent decline in peer review quality across computer science conferences over time?
kuznetsov2026_peer_review.data_source: Peer review data from ICLR, NeurIPS, and ACL Rolling Review (ARR) conferences.
conclusion_claims: Contrary to the popular narrative, there is no consistent decline in median review quality across venues and years.
The diversity of review formats and the evolution of reviewing workflows make it challenging to compare reviews across time and venues.
limitations: The data is scarce, biased, and noisy.
The diversity and evolution of reviewing workflows make consistent application of review quality measures difficult.

partial_results.metric: impact of LLMs on review quality
partial_results.note: The impact of LLMs on review quality is not fully quantified due to limited data on their use in reviewing campaigns.

parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.title: Towards A Sustainable Future for Peer Review in Software Engineering
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.venue: 2026 IEEE/ACM 48th International Conference on Software Engineering: Future of Software Engineering (ICSE-FoSE)
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.research_question: How can the software engineering research community ensure a sustainable and high-quality peer review process in the face of increasing submission volumes and reviewer overload?
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.data_source: The paper references data from software engineering conferences (ICSE, FSE, ASE, ICSME, MSR, SANER) regarding program committee sizes and submission volumes over the last five years.
conclusion_claims: The current imbalance between submission volume and reviewer availability is not sustainable and risks harming the quality of peer review in software engineering.
A sustainable future for peer review requires expanding the pool of qualified reviewers through training and mentorship programs, incentivizing participation, and cautiously integrating AI tools.
limitations: The paper is based on descriptive analysis and does not include quantitative evaluation of proposed solutions.
The impact of AI tools on the peer review process is not empirically tested.

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide quantitative results on the impact of AI tools on the review process, as this is a proposed future direction rather than an evaluated mechanism.

bogner2026_state_peer_review.title: The State of Peer Review in Empirical Software Engineering: A Community Survey on Review Load, Quality, and GenAI Use
bogner2026_state_peer_review.research_question: What is the current state of peer review in the ESE community, including perceived review load, review quality, challenges, and the use of LLM-based tools, and what suggestions do community members have for improvement?
bogner2026_state_peer_review.data_source: Questionnaire survey responses collected from ESE researchers and reviewers.
conclusion_claims: The ESE community is experiencing high review loads, with most participants rating their review load as high or very high.
Review quality is a concern, with challenges such as superficial evaluations and inconsistency.
The use of LLMs in the reviewing process is a growing concern, with potential impacts on review integrity and depth.
Community suggestions for improvement include adopting double-anonymous or open reviewing models, structured review forms, and reviewer training programs.
limitations: The sample is skewed towards senior researchers from Europe and North America, which may limit the generalizability of the findings.
The survey did not systematically quantify the extent and impact of LLM use in the ESE review process.
The study is based on self-reported data, which may be subject to social desirability bias.

result_claims.value: High or very high for 80% of participants
Average of 25-32 reviews per person per year
Not explicitly quantified but discussed as a concern

partial_results.note: The extent and impact of LLM use in ESE review processes were not systematically quantified in the survey.

ebrahimi2026_peeriscope.venue: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW Companion '26)
ebrahimi2026_peeriscope.research_question: How can peer review quality be systematically, interpretable, and extensibly assessed using a multi-faceted framework?
conclusion_claims: PeeriScope provides a multi-faceted framework for evaluating peer review quality, integrating structured features, LLM-based assessments, and supervised prediction.
PeeriScope is designed for openness and integration, offering a public interface and a documented API for practical deployment and research extensibility.
limitations: Peer review datasets are inherently sparse, fragmented across venues, and largely inaccessible due to confidentiality constraints.
Evaluating LLM-assisted practices remains difficult due to the lack of annotated, high-quality review data.

wu2025_large_language_models.title: Large language models for post-publication research evaluation: Evidence from expert recommendations and citation indicators
wu2025_large_language_models.research_question: To what extent can LLMs perform post-publication research evaluation tasks in comparison with expert judgments, and how do model selection and learning strategies influence performance?
conclusion_claims: LLMs demonstrate strong performance in coarse-grained evaluation tasks but struggle with fine-grained stratification of article quality.
Supervised fine-tuning produces the strongest and most balanced results for post-publication research evaluation.
limitations: The study is limited to the biomedical field and the H1 Connect platform.
The closed-source nature of GPT-5 prevents further analysis of its performance advantages.

result_claims.value: improved with few-shot prompting and supervised fine-tuning

partial_results.note: Semantically similar examples do not consistently strengthen alignment with citation-based indicators.

kousha2025_stylistic_features.title: Which stylistic features fool ChatGPT research evaluations?
kousha2025_stylistic_features.research_question: Which textual features of abstracts (e.g., readability, linguistic complexity, and length) associate with LLM scores, and are these associations stronger for LLM scores than for REF expert scores?
conclusion_claims: Linguistic complexity and length of abstracts are more strongly associated with ChatGPT scores than with REF expert scores in many subject areas.
ChatGPT may be more likely than human experts to reward linguistic complexity, with a potential bias towards longer and less readable abstracts in many fields.
limitations: Cause-and-effect relationships between abstract features and scores were not tested.
The use of departmental average scores as a proxy for individual article quality may introduce inaccuracies.

yadav2026_project_synapse.research_question: How can a hierarchical, memory-augmented agentic architecture effectively manage and resolve complex, real-time disruptions in last-mile delivery operations?

The hierarchical, memory-augmented agentic architecture is viable for dynamic, real-world problem-solving in high-stakes operational environments.

hao2024_hlm_cite.title: HLM-Cite: Hybrid Language Model Workflow for Text-based Scientific Citation Prediction
hao2024_hlm_cite.venue: 38th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2024)
hao2024_hlm_cite.research_question: How can we accurately predict core citations in scientific papers by distinguishing them from superficial citations and non-citations, especially when dealing with large candidate sets?
conclusion_claims: HLM-Cite improves citation prediction by distinguishing core citations from superficial citations and non-citations.
The hybrid workflow enables the model to scale to large candidate sets (up to 100K papers) while maintaining high precision.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention limitations in the provided text.
The evaluation is based on a single dataset (Microsoft Academic Graph), which may limit generalizability.

result_claims.significance: Thousands of times more than existing works

partial_results.note: The paper provides statistical evidence that core citations are more closely related to the query paper in terms of keyword overlap and frequency of mentions.

wu2026_geographical_biases.title: Persistent geographical biases in global scientific collaboration and citations
wu2026_geographical_biases.research_question: How do geographic distance and country-level factors shape international scientific collaboration and citation?
conclusion_claims: Geographic distance has a stronger effect on collaboration than on citations, and this effect has intensified over time.
Persistent spatial and country biases in global science suggest structural or institutional barriers to equitable participation and recognition.
limitations: The study relies on bibliometric data, which may not capture all aspects of scientific interaction.
The analysis is limited to city-level data, which may not fully represent individual-level dynamics.

partial_results.note: The study notes that China is systematically undercited despite being a favored collaboration partner.

bank2026_data_driven_analysis.title: A Data-Driven Analysis for Engineering Conferences: The Institute of Industrial and Systems Engineering (IISE) Annual Conference Proceedings (2002–2025)
bank2026_data_driven_analysis.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 IISE Annual Conference
bank2026_data_driven_analysis.research_question: What is the intellectual evolution of the Industrial and Systems Engineering (ISE) field as reflected in the IISE Annual Conference proceedings from 2002 to 2025?
conclusion_claims: The study provides a comprehensive cartography of the ISE field's intellectual history, identifying dominant, emerging, and receding research topics.
The integration of ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets, along with LLM-based classification and network analysis, offers valuable insights into knowledge diffusion and community structure in ISE.
limitations: The study relies on automated classification methods, which may introduce errors in identifying research domains and sub-areas.
The citation data from Google Scholar may have quality and reproducibility issues, which could affect the accuracy of impact analysis.

result_claims.value: Dominant, emerging, and receding research topics were identified
Influential papers and authors were identified

partial_results.note: The accuracy of LLM-based classification was not explicitly reported, but it was stated that open-source LLMs combined with semantic search can extract domain-specific knowledge with over 90% accuracy.

peng2026_structural_diversity.title: Structural Diversity Drives Disruptive Scientific Innovation
peng2026_structural_diversity.research_question: What organizational structure fosters disruptive scientific innovation, and how does Structural Diversity (SD) influence this process?
conclusion_claims: Structural Diversity (SD) is a powerful and robust predictor of disruptive innovation, outperforming traditional team novelty indicators.
SD positively interacts with team size, transforming team scale from a liability into a resource for creative synthesis.
limitations: The study is based on a historical dataset of scientific publications, which may not capture the full complexity of current collaborative dynamics.
The CD Index used to measure disruptive innovation may not fully capture all dimensions of scientific impact.

result_claims.metric: Interaction between SD and Team Size

partial_results.metric: Effect of SD on CD Index
partial_results.note: The effect of SD on CD Index is robust even after controlling for key confounding factors.

zheng2026_citerag.title: What Should I Cite? A RAG Benchmark for Academic Citation Prediction
zheng2026_citerag.venue: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW ’26)
zheng2026_citerag.research_question: How can a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework be designed and evaluated to effectively predict academic citations at both coarse-grained and fine-grained levels?
conclusion_claims: The proposed multi-level hybrid RAG approach is effective for academic citation prediction at both coarse-grained and fine-grained levels.
The CiteRAG benchmark provides a comprehensive evaluation framework for citation prediction and serves as a methodological template for other scientific domains.

lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.title: From Newborn to Impact: Bias-Aware Citation Prediction
lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.research_question: How can we design bias-aware models that deliver stable predictions on low-citation papers while revealing the underlying drivers of scientific impact?
conclusion_claims: The proposed Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) achieves significant improvements in citation prediction for newborn papers.
The model reduces error metrics by around 13% and improves the ranking metric by 5.5% compared to baseline methods.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed information about the time window of the data.
The paper does not discuss the generalizability of the model to other domains or types of academic content.

petersen2024_disruption_index.title: The disruption index suffers from citation inflation and is confounded by shifts in scholarly citation practice
petersen2024_disruption_index.research_question: Is the disruption index (CD) a reliable measure of innovation trends, or is it confounded by secular growth and citation practices?
petersen2024_disruption_index.data_source: SciSciNet database, PNAS publications (2011-2015), and prior studies (e.g., Wu et al., Park et al.)
conclusion_claims: The disruption index (CD) is susceptible to measurement error due to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices.
The relationship between CD and team size is not consistently negative and shifts to positive for larger teams.
limitations: The study relies on observational data and quasi-experimental designs, which may not fully account for all confounding variables.
The results are based on a specific citation window and may not generalize to other time periods or citation practices.

result_claims.metric: relationship between CD and team size
result_claims.value: shifts from negative to positive for team size ≥8

result_claims.metric: effect size of temporal and team-size trends

partial_results.note: The observed decline is attributed to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices, not to a decline in innovation.

petersen2023_citation_inflation.title: The disruption index is biased by citation inflation
petersen2023_citation_inflation.research_question: Is the observed decline in the disruption index over time a true reflection of reduced innovation or an artifact of citation inflation?
petersen2023_citation_inflation.data_source: Microsoft Academic Graph (MAG) citation network, Clarivate Analytics Web of Science
conclusion_claims: The disruption index is systematically biased by citation inflation, making it unsuitable for cross-temporal analysis of scientific and technological disruption.
Citation inflation is driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which increase the density of citation networks and confound measures of disruption.
limitations: The analysis is based on descriptive statistics and computational modeling rather than controlled experiments.
The paper does not provide a fully validated alternative to the disruption index for cross-temporal analysis.

result_claims.value: grows exponentially with an annual rate of 0.051

partial_results.note: The decline in the disruption index is attributed to citation inflation rather than a true decline in innovation.

kozlowski2023_uncited_articles.title: Uncited articles and their effect on the concentration of citations
kozlowski2023_uncited_articles.research_question: Are citations becoming more concentrated in a small number of articles, or are they becoming more decentralized due to recent geopolitical and technical changes in science?
conclusion_claims: Citations are becoming more scattered over time, contrary to the widely-held belief that they are becoming more concentrated.
The decline in citation concentration is primarily driven by a reduction in the number of uncited articles, which is influenced by the growing significance of Asia and Europe.
limitations: The analysis is based on the Web of Science, which may not fully represent all scholarly outputs, especially in the social sciences and humanities.
The study does not account for the full complexity of citation practices, such as the role of non-English publications or open-access availability.

partial_results.note: The observed decrease in citation concentration is partially attributed to the inclusion of uncited articles in the analysis.

alSinani2025_aiGeneratedPapers.title: From Content Creation to Citation Inflation: A GenAI Case Study
alSinani2025_aiGeneratedPapers.research_question: Are there fully AI-generated papers on ResearchGate, particularly in the area of GenAI-enhanced Cybersecurity, and if so, how are they being used to manipulate academic metrics?
conclusion_claims: AI-generated papers can bypass platform checks and remain publicly accessible on ResearchGate.
These papers contribute to inflating citation metrics such as the H-index and i10-index.
limitations: The study is limited to a single platform (ResearchGate) and a specific research area (GenAI-enhanced Cybersecurity).
The controlled experiment involved a single fake paper and may not generalize to all AI-generated papers.

partial_results.note: Limited to manual checks and may not be comprehensive

banerjee2025_self_citation_adjusted.research_question: How can citation metrics be adjusted to account for self-citation patterns in order to provide a more accurate and equitable assessment of research impact?
banerjee2025_self_citation_adjusted.data_source: Not explicitly mentioned, but likely from open-access bibliometric databases.
conclusion_claims: The Self-Citation Adjusted Index (SCAI) provides a more accurate and equitable assessment of research impact by accounting for self-citation patterns.
Adjusting for self-citation can reduce the gender citation gap and lead to more equitable research funding allocation.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed information about the specific datasets or databases used for the analysis.
The paper does not present statistical significance tests for the observed effects.

partial_results.note: The paper references prior studies but does not present original longitudinal data on the long-term impact of self-citation patterns.

wu2026_persistent_geographical_biases.title: Persistent geographical biases in global scientific collaboration and citations
wu2026_persistent_geographical_biases.research_question: How do geographic distance and country-level factors shape international scientific collaboration and citation?
conclusion_claims: Geographic distance has a stronger effect on collaboration than on citations, and this effect has intensified over time.
Persistent spatial and country biases in global science suggest structural or institutional barriers to equitable participation and recognition.
limitations: The study focuses on city-level interactions and may not capture finer-grained or individual-level effects.
The analysis is based on a single dataset (OpenAlex), which may have coverage or representation biases.

partial_results.note: The study notes that China is systematically undercited despite being a favored collaboration partner.

ke2023_normalized_impact.title: A network-based normalized impact measure reveals successful periods of scientific discovery across disciplines
ke2023_normalized_impact.research_question: Can a cocitation-network-based citation normalization eliminate temporal and field biases in citation counts and reveal successful periods of scientific discovery across all disciplines?
ke2023_normalized_impact.data_source: Web of Science (WoS) via Clarivate Analytics
conclusion_claims: Ĉ provides a universal, time- and field-invariant citation impact measure without requiring pre-defined discipline labels
Cocitation-defined comparison sets automatically capture a paper's local research context, outperforming field-label-based normalization
Ĉ reveals successful periods of discovery in smaller fields (geosciences, radiology, optics) that are systematically overlooked by raw citation counts
The methodology enables fair comparison of scientific impact across diverse fields and time periods
limitations: Relies on Web of Science coverage; may underrepresent non-English or non-journal literature
Cocitation data requires reference lists; older papers with incomplete reference data may be affected
T=10 citation window may disadvantage very recent papers with insufficient citation accumulation
Does not address citation motives (positive vs. negative citations)

result_claims.significance: KS-test: Ĉ shows no field bias

result_claims.value: Ĉ ranks milestone papers consistently higher than C_raw
result_claims.significance: Milestone rank improvement: higher percentiles under Ĉ

partial_results.note: Limited to papers indexed by NIH iCite; Ĉ has broader coverage
Main results shown for T=10 only; appendix has T=5,15,20

N_i,t = set of papers cocited with i by citing papers published in year t. \u27e8c_n,t\u27e9 = mean yearly citations of papers in N_i,t."}, {"name": "V4", "type": "count", "formula": "C_i,T = \u03a3_{t=0}^T c_i,t, total raw citations over T years"}, {"name": "V5", "type": "continuous", "formula": "C\u0303_i,T = C_i,T / \u27e8C_T\u27e9_field, normalized by average citations of papers in same year and journal-based field"}]

Dependent variable formula: Ĉ_i,T = Σ_{t=0}^T ĉ_i,t where ĉ_i,t = c_i,t / ⟨c_n,t⟩_{n∈N_i,t}. c_i,t = yearly raw citations to paper i in year t. N_i,t = set of papers cocited with i by citing papers published in year t. ⟨c_n,t⟩ = mean yearly citations of papers in N_i,t.
Dependent variable formula: C_i,T = Σ_{t=0}^T c_i,t, total raw citations over T years

arts2025_frontier_scientists.title: Not like the others: Frontier scientists for inventive performance
arts2025_frontier_scientists.research_question: Do corporate patents invented by frontier scientists have higher technological impact and private value compared to patents by other inventors, and what mechanisms explain this premium?
arts2025_frontier_scientists.data_source: USPTO, PubMed, Microsoft Academic Graph, OpenAlex, CRSP/Compustat
conclusion_claims: Corporate patents by frontier scientists have significantly higher technology impact than patents by non-author inventors and non-frontier authors
The frontier-author premium is strongest in young scaled-up biopharmaceutical firms and for internally employed frontier scientists
Greater use of frontier science as prior art partially explains but does not fully account for the frontier-author premium
Recency and top-general nature of the science matter: the premium weakens when using lower-tier journals or longer publication windows
limitations: Analysis limited to biomedical sector; findings may not generalize to other industries (e.g., software, chemicals)
Selection on unobservables: frontier authors may select into firms/projects with inherently higher innovative potential
Author-inventor disambiguation may have false positives/negatives despite using validated linkage methods
Kogan private value data available for only 36% of sample (public firms only)
Causal interpretation limited: despite FE and matching, unobserved time-varying confounders may remain
Frontier science definition based on top-general journals may miss important frontier work in specialized outlets

result_claims.significance: Frontier-author premium persists even without frontier refs

partial_results.note: Premiums exist for both frontier-science-referencing and non-referencing patents; full mechanism remains partially unexplained
Sample size limited for externally-employed frontier author analysis; results suggestive but not conclusive
Analysis limited to biomedical sector; generalizability to other industries untested
